subcategory: spectacularly bicolored South African Cape corm

planting.note: Plant small corms 2–3 inches deep in very well-drained, lean to slightly acidic, sandy soil in full sun; wine cups follows the Cape winter-rainfall growth cycle — emerging in autumn with rains, blooming in late winter and spring (August–October in South Africa, February–April in the Northern Hemisphere), then going completely summer dormant. Keep bone dry in summer dormancy. One of the most spectacularly colored small Cape corms. Best in containers in areas with wet summers.

directions: Plant corms in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic, sandy soil in full sun; wine cups is one of the most dramatically, improbably beautiful small Cape corms — a botanical rarity that produces flowers of such striking, sharply contrasting bicolor perfection that the first sight of a blooming plant reliably produces disbelief. Each flower is a shallow, open, wide-faced cup (the 'wine cup') of pure, deep, vivid blue-violet to royal blue — and at the center of each petal, precisely positioned, a bold, dark wine-red to maroon-red blotch that creates a starburst pattern of wine-red at the center of the blue cup, the two colors absolutely saturated and sharply demarcated in a bicolor combination of electric blue and blood-red that appears painted by an artist rather than produced by a plant. The common name 'Sentry-in-a-Box' refers to the traditional observation that the red markings look like a small figure standing in a blue sentry box. Native to a small area of the Western Cape around Darling in the sandy coastal fynbos, it is one of the Cape's many microendemic botanical wonders — a plant of restricted range but astonishing floral beauty. Outstanding in pots and alpine house conditions where summer dryness is easily controlled; a collector's gem of the highest order.

planting.note: Plant corms 3–4 inches deep in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; blue butterfly moraea flowers in autumn (September–November in the Southern Hemisphere, blooming in autumn/early winter in cultivation in the Northern Hemisphere) unlike the spring-blooming pavonia moraeas. It is a summer-rainfall South African species — not a Cape winter-rainfall species — and therefore more tolerant of summer moisture than many other moraeas. The flowers last only a single day but are produced in succession.

directions: Plant corms in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; blue butterfly moraea is notable for two unusual characteristics: it flowers in autumn rather than spring (unlike most iris-family South African plants), and the flowers are a vivid, clear, mid-to-deep blue — a color that is striking in any plant but particularly valuable in the autumn flower season when pure-blue flowers are rare. The individual blooms are typical moraea form — three large outer falls with a yellow, black, and white 'nectar guide' marking at the base, three smaller inner standards, all in clear blue-violet to mid-blue — and each lasts only a single day, but the corm produces multiple flowering stems and each stem multiple flowers in succession over several weeks of autumn bloom. The tall, branching, airy flowering stems rise well above the single, cylindrical, grass-like leaf. It is a summer-rainfall grassland species from the Drakensberg foothills and eastern South Africa, giving it better tolerance of summer rain than the Cape winter-rainfall species, and making it more practical for the zone 9 Gulf Coast autumn garden where rainfall continues through summer and fall. An exceptional autumn blue for zones 8–11.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring or plant container-grown plants in moist but well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; New Zealand iris is evergreen and provides structural foliage through the year. It tolerates both dry and seasonally moist conditions once established. The orange-tinted winter foliage (in some forms intensifying to bright orange in cold weather) is a distinctive seasonal feature. Named 'mikoikoi' in Maori.

directions: Plant in moist but well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; New Zealand iris is a structural, evergreen, iris-family perennial from New Zealand that provides year-round interest through two distinct phases: the foliage and the flowers. The sword-shaped, narrow, bright-green to orange-tinted leaves are held in the classic iris-family flat fan arrangement, and in cool weather (particularly winter) the leaves often intensify to bright orange, golden-orange, or bronze-orange in some forms, creating a warm, glowing winter-foliage effect that is particularly striking in a bare-winter border. In spring through early summer the plant produces branching, airy panicles of small, white, three-outer-petaled flowers — each individual flower small, pure white, and open-faced, collectively creating a light, airy, fine-textured spray of white above the structural foliage. The combination of the structural, strap-like fans of orange-tinged leaves with the sprays of white flowers above them is architecturally satisfying throughout the year. The Maori name 'mikoikoi' acknowledges the plant's deep cultural history in New Zealand. Outstanding in zone 9 Gulf Coast borders as a year-round structural foliage plant with spring flowers.

planting.note: Plant small corms 2–3 inches deep in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or gritty soil in full sun; romulea flowers in late winter to early spring (February–April) in sunny weather — the flowers close at night and in cloudy or cold weather, opening only when the sun reaches them. The whole plant goes summer dormant. Common romulea is widespread across the Mediterranean Basin from Portugal to Turkey, giving it wide climatic tolerance across zones 7–10.

directions: Plant corms in very well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or gritty soil in full sun; common romulea is a small but charming Mediterranean iris-family plant — one of about 90 Romulea species distributed between the Mediterranean Basin and southern Africa — that produces in late winter and early spring (February–April in the Mediterranean, opening only in sunny weather and closing at night) cup-shaped to star-shaped flowers in shades of pale to deep lilac-violet to purple, with yellow at the center throat and often with darker veining on the outer surface of the tepals. The flowers are small — 1–1.5 inches across — but produced in such abundance on a naturalized colony that the collective display of small, lilac crocus-like cups opening to the sun is very pretty. The narrow, rush-like leaves, emerging before the flowers, are quill-like and wiry. The whole plant goes completely summer dormant. It is particularly interesting among Mediterranean bulbs for blooming so early — in Feb–March among the earliest of all spring bulbs — and for the behavior of flower opening only in direct sunlight. Naturalizes freely in lean, well-drained, sunny soils of rock gardens and gravel paths.

planting.note: Plant small bulbs 2–3 inches deep in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Kunth's wild onion is native to the rocky, limestone-based hills, prairies, and open woodlands of Texas, New Mexico, Arizona, and northern Mexico, where it blooms in spring and early summer. The bulbs are edible — both leaves and bulbs — and have been used as a food plant by indigenous peoples of the Southwest and Mexico for centuries. The strong, garlicky-onion scent distinguishes it readily from non-edible similar plants.

directions: Plant small bulbs in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Kunth's wild onion is one of the most widespread and important edible native alliums of Texas and the American Southwest — a spring-blooming, small wild onion native to the limestone hills, Rocky Mountain foothills, and Chihuahuan Desert mountains of Texas, New Mexico, Arizona, and northern Mexico, where it has been harvested and eaten by indigenous peoples for thousands of years as a wild food plant. The bulbs and leaves both have a strong onion-garlic flavor and aroma, usable in the same ways as domestic onions and garlic — raw, cooked, pickled. The spring flowers are produced in small, rounded umbels of white to pale pink to deep pink star-shaped florets on stems 8–16 inches tall, serving as pollinators for native bees and beetles. In the garden it provides both a wildflower display in spring and an edible wild-food plant for harvesting — a dual-purpose native species for the Texas-Southwest edible landscape and native plant garden. The strong garlic-onion scent immediately identifies it as edible and distinguishes it safely from the non-scented, potentially toxic Nothoscordum bivalve (crow poison) with which it sometimes grows.
